explanations.8: This line exits the function early if the 'target' variable is empty, which prevents the function from running with no host to work with.
explanations.12: This uses 'fzf' (a fuzzy finder tool) to show a menu with three options, then stores whichever option the user picks into the 'cmd' variable.
explanations.14: This extracts the actual hostname from SSH config by running 'ssh -G' (which dumps all SSH settings for that host) and finding the line that starts with 'hostname'.
explanations.24: This reads a single keypress from the user without echoing it to the screen, so it can wait silently for them to press any key.
explanations.65: The code stores ';; ' as a separator string that will be used later to join multiple comment lines together.
explanations.66: This removes Windows-style line endings (carriage returns) and strips the newline from the end of each line.
explanations.70: This strips the '#' comment character and any whitespace after it from the beginning of a line, leaving just the comment text.
explanations.80: When a new Host entry is found, the code prints out the previous host's info (if any exists) and then resets the variables to start collecting info for the new host.
explanations.95: The code pads the output with 150 spaces between the hostname column and the description separator, so descriptions don't visually overlap with the hostname list.
explanations.113: This launches Perl to convert the special color tags (like '<red>') into ANSI escape codes that terminals understand for displaying colors.
explanations.124: When fzf is run with '--expect', it outputs the pressed key on the first line and the selected item on the second line, so this extracts that key.
explanations.125: This extracts the second line of fzf's output, which contains the full row the user selected from the list.
explanations.126: This grabs just the first column (the alias name) from the selected row by splitting on whitespace.
explanations.133: This runs 'ssh -G' with the alias name, which expands it to show all the real SSH settings that would actually be used (like the real username, real hostname, and key file).
